their executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note if you would like

conclusions as well as what's specific

Market risk need to be a client of 4
macro so today's kcro question is how
inevitable is a too strong US dollar
part two our answers are there are
severable several probable economic and
policy outcomes that are likely to
support the current uptrend in the US
dollar over the medium term including
but not limited to number one Widing
interest rate differentials number two
tariffs and number three Doge a wild
card in our long-term dollar bull thesis
is the likely 7 to800 billion of te
treasury general account spin down that
we currently expect to occur amid a debt
caling impass early next year that
outcome should be dollar bearish and
support risk assets through late q1 or
early Q2 beyond that the replenishing of
the TGA amid excessive Global
refinancing demand may contribute to a
global refinancing air pocket that will
be akin to borrowers playing musical
chairs with the limited dollars of
available balance sheet capacity it is
also likely that treasury incoming
treasury secretary Scott bessent has
shifted uh us net financing policy in a
honkus manner by then as well refer to
the left teror sections of our December
2024 macros scouting report as well as
our November 30th uh December 7th 14th
and 21st Around the Horn webcast for the
institutional grade analysis supporting
these conclusions as well as the
implications for your portfolio
transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
here as always a wrap up with a question
from our community uh this one's
regarding fake QT versus real QT says
hello ddl what is the uh when the FED
started reducing its balance sheet that
is supposed to drain liquidity from the
monetary system I'm assuming the FED can
do do does this as a way to curb
inflation while also raising the
interest rate is this fake QT because
they were not hawkish enough because ass
prices skyrocketed GDP was good
unemployment was low and they bailed out
the banks
again if if it were real QT the FED
balance sheet would like be somewhere uh
below where we were a few years ago and
asset prices would grow more slowly
around 10% perom GDP growth would be
lower unemployment would be higher am I
understanding real QT and fake QT
accurately uh so excellent question one
of my favorite topics um you know sort
of uh grab a beer and talk about macro
topics you know at 5m is a sort of
concept of of fake QT versus real QT um
you know when we talk about so this is a
great question and um the spoiler alert
is that the uh the clients your sort of
interpretation of the outcome of fake QT
uh as it relates to a more resilient
economy very buoyant asset prices you
know a very resilient labor market those
are the outcomes of implementing fake QT
but what fake QT actually is is balance
sheet runoff and so if you recall like
when the FED does quantitative easing
it's actually expanding its balance
sheet to acquire security ities from the
secondary markets in order to uh uh
enact monetary policy to to to to create
balance sheet capacity for the global
private uh sector uh uh whether it be
Banks or or non-banks uh to so that they
can lever up and buy more assets and and
keep the system going when the FED does
uh so in theory if that if buying the
assets from the private sector is
quantitative easing then it Sur we
should surmise that quantitative
tightening is selling assets to the
private sector well the reason we call
it fake QT is because the FED has not
sold any bonds to the private sector the
FED is simply allowing treasury
Securities that are maturing to roll off
its balance sheet and rather than uh re
um rather than uh repurchasing those uh
Securities uh in order to maintain the
same size of a balance sheet is just
allowing its balance sheet to gradually
shrink as a function of that process you
know so you know even if the FED wanted
to maintain the same size of the balance
sheet it can't do that in a static way
like if you're own a bunch of 10year
Treasury Securities 10 years from now
those 10year treasury Securities will be
you know zero DTE treasury Securities
and once they expire the balance sheet
would shrink otherwise unless you of
course um um Le the V of course bought
those bought incremental treasury
Securities in that regard so the FED
can't just maintain a static balance
sheet in the context of a fixed income
Market that is obviously structured
across various teners and durations and
so when the FED does fqt AKA balance
sheet runoff all it's doing is saying
okay we're allowing this this this
treasury security to roll off our
security well off our balance sheet but
we're not going to replace it in order
to maintain the same size of balance
sheet so we're going to gradually allow
our balance sheet to Decay and so in our
opinion this this this uh is is a step
in the hawkish direction but it's not an
exactly a particularly hawkish uh thing
in especially in the context of um of
what the the expiring debt debt you know
the expiring piece of a treasury paper
allows for the rest the rest of the
private sector you know as the treasury
paper expires and obviously unless it's
being replaced by the um by the fiscal
Authority which of course it is we're
running a budget deficit but in
isolation if it wasn't being replaced by
the fiscal Authority terms of new
borrowing then you would actually be
creating liquidity uh in the private
sector but again that's not the
condition now because again we have
these massive fiscal deficits as well so
the key takeaway is that because the FED
is not selling bonds to the market it
did not tighten Financial conditions
nearly as much as you would think it
would in a quantitative tightening
scenario and as in function of that
we've had a significantly more resilient
economy more resilient labor market
significantly buoyant asset prices as a
function of all that and other Dynamics
as well right you know the FED balance
sheet is not the only reason the economy
has been resilient there are a lot of
reasons why the econom is resilient that
are irrespective of what's happened on
the FED balance sheets so I want to make
sure investors are well aware of that
and um one final thing just

from our our community here uh this one
